macro_segments.title: Introduction to the Mishna: The Talking Cow and Nazir Status

micro_segments.title: Opening the Mishna: A cow declares itself a nazir if it stands
The dispute between Beit Shammai and Beit Hillel on the cow's statement
Rabbi Yehuda's interpretation: the cow is forbidden, not actual nazirut

macro_segments.title: Gemara's Initial Response: Questioning Animal Speech and Proposing Reframing

micro_segments.title: Rami bar Chama's explanation: the cow is crouched, unable to stand
Reframing the case: I will be a nazir from the meat if it stands
Why this case repeats the Beit Shammai/Beit Hillel debate about dates

macro_segments.title: Why Repeat the Debate: The Multiple Illustrations of Beit Shammai and Beit Hillel

micro_segments.title: The Gemara acknowledges repetition: Beit Shammai stated this position multiple times
Explaining dates: grapes and dates are confused because they are similar
Meat does not confuse with grapes; therefore the dates case needed explanation
The association of wine and meat as sensual indulgence in nazirut
A door as the most absurd case: why include it if some cases make sense

macro_segments.title: Tosafot's Interpretive Approaches: Making Sense of the Anthropomorphic Cow

micro_segments.title: Tosafot's first approach: anthropomorphism and the mapik hey reading
Correcting the grammar: hareini nezirah becomes I am a nazir from it
Tosafot's second approach: treating the cow story literally and reinterpreting
The cow's story as mere narrative: what the person really means is nazirut

macro_segments.title: Rava's Alternative Explanation: The Condition Reframed

micro_segments.title: Rava's objection: the Mishna lacks the word 'of its own' if the cow stood naturally
Rava's reframing: you said 'if it doesn't stand,' not 'if it does stand'
Beit Shammai's view: 'if I can't make it stand' versus 'if it doesn't stand'
Beit Hillel's view: focus is only on the crouching; the cow stood—condition not met

macro_segments.title: Resolving the Debate: How Rava's Explanation Fits Rabbi Yehuda's Position

micro_segments.title: Problem: if you explicitly said 'nazir from wine,' why does Rabbi Yehuda limit it to forbidden meat?
Solution: the case must be 'I am a nazir from its meat,' not 'from wine'
Beit Shammai concedes: even you (Beit Hillel) should agree the cow stood—condition not met
Beit Hillel's counter: your condition was about my ability to raise it, which I didn't do

macro_segments.title: The Simplest Reading: The Talking Cow as Distraction from Core Dispute

micro_segments.title: Core claim: the talking cow is window dressing for 'I will be a nazir from its meat if it stands'
Why the Gemara uses multiple illustrations: to show the debate applies even in absurd cases
Conclusion: the Mishna parallels the dates case with the same three positions

topical_tags.term: nazir from specific objects (dates, meat, door)
Beit Shammai position: declares person is a nazir
Beit Hillel position: declares person is not a nazir
Rabbi Yehuda: the object is forbidden, not actual nazirut
anthropomorphism: reading human intent into animal/object statements
mapik hey: the final ה as a suffix meaning 'from it'
Rami bar Chama: cow crouched, unable to stand naturally
grapes and dates confused; both are fruit
wine and meat as sensual indulgence linked in nazirut
door as an absurd example of nazir condition
condition-based vow: if X happens, then I am a nazir
turpiya: the intent or focus of the person making the condition
okmei biyadei: ability to raise/lift the cow stands
Tosafot: interpretive reconciliation of the Mishna with the Gemara
pshat: literal meaning of the Mishna text
chiddush: novel ruling or interpretation in the Mishna
repeated cases in debate illustrate the principle even in absurd circumstances
translation/interpretation of absurd vow as meaningful intent
